title: Workshop: IS-LM Shocks in the Real World
summary: Small-group discussion of fiscal vs monetary shocks using IS-LM. Bring the chart from the lesson and use the four canonical presets as anchors.

questions.prompt: Pick a recent macro headline (2023 onward). Argue which curve shifted and in which direction. Your group must agree on a single answer before moving on.
questions.notes: Instructor: nudge groups toward separating supply-side from demand-side stories. A common error is attributing inflation to AD when the trigger was a supply shock (e.g. 2022 oil).

questions.prompt: The Fed cuts the policy rate by 50 bp. Two groups split: which one finds the predicted (Y, r) effect using IS-LM, and which one finds it using AD-AS? Compare answers.
questions.notes: Bridges the two short-run models. IS-LM gives (Y up, r down). AD-AS gives (Y up, P up). The two are consistent because AD shifts right when r falls.

questions.prompt: If Congress passes a $200B fiscal stimulus while the Fed simultaneously tightens by an equal real-purchasing-power amount, what happens to Y? To r? Are the two policies interchangeable?
questions.notes: Y effects can cancel; r unambiguously rises (both push it up). Borrowers (homebuyers, businesses, government itself) face higher rates than under either policy alone.

questions.prompt: Identify one mechanism in your textbook that IS-LM is too simple to capture. How would you extend the model? (Examples: expectations, financial frictions, open economy.)
questions.notes: Encourages metacognition about model assumptions. Acceptable extensions: New Keynesian Phillips curve, financial accelerator, Mundell-Fleming, ZLB.

questions.prompt: Group draws the post-2020 path of (Y, r) on a single IS-LM diagram, labeling each phase. Compare across groups.
questions.notes: 2020-Q2 IS shifts left dramatically; Fed shifts LM right (ZLB). 2021 IS recovers fast (fiscal). 2022 LM shifts left (tightening). 2024 ambiguous.

questions.prompt: What does 'crowding out' look like in IS-LM? Find the slider/preset combination that produces the most crowding out. What policy mix minimizes it?
questions.notes: High G + low M produces strong crowding out. High G + accommodative M (rises with G) minimizes it. Tie to actual Treasury-Fed coordination debates.
